---
title: "Tarflower (Bejaria racemosa)"
slug: "bejaria-racemosa"
group: "Plantae"
image: "https://inaturalist-open-data.s3.amazonaws.com/photos/476397646/medium.jpg"        # keep for thumbnails
image-alt: "Tarflower"  # keep for a11y
tax_kingdom: "Plantae"
tax_class: "Magnoliopsida"
tax_order: "Ericales"
tax_family: "Ericaceae"
categories: ["Plantae", "Magnoliopsida", "Ericales", "Ericaceae"]   # these drive the chips + filtering
freeze: true
---

<figure class="species-figure">

<img src="https://inaturalist-open-data.s3.amazonaws.com/photos/476397646/medium.jpg" alt="Tarflower" class="species-photo"/>

<figcaption class="photo-credit">

Photo: <a href="https://www.inaturalist.org/people/garccyy">
garccyy</a> (CC BY-NC) via <a href="https://www.inaturalist.org/observations/265241400">iNaturalist</a>

</figcaption>

</figure>

## Taxonomy

-   **Class:** Magnoliopsida
-   **Order:** Ericales
-   **Family:** Ericaceae

## Occurrence at DeLuca Preserve

-   [DeLuca Bio Blitz Species Observations](https://www.inaturalist.org/observations?project_id=191727&subview=map&taxon_id=126342)

## Conservation & rarity context

-   IUCN: Not Listed
-   **Rarity note:** Common species in Florida found in dry, sandy habitats.

## Natural history notes

An evergreen shrub characterized by its narrow leaves and showy pink to rose-colored flowers with sticky, glandular surfaces. In Florida, it is found in dry, sandy habitats such as scrub, flatwoods, and pine barrens. It typically blooms in late winter to spring and is well adapted to nutrient-poor soils, often occurring in fire-maintained ecosystems.

## References / links

-   [iNaturalist species page](https://www.inaturalist.org/taxa/126342-Bejaria-racemosa)
-   [Local profile](https://www.flawildflowers.org/flower-friday-bejaria-racemosa/)
